questions.question: In the CAP theorem framing used in distributed systems interviews, why is partition tolerance (P) treated as non-negotiable at scale?
options: Network partitions will happen in real deployments, so the design must continue operating despite split connectivity
Partition tolerance means all nodes share one physical rack
P requires sacrificing availability in every normal operating mode
P only applies to single-datacenter LANs, not the public Internet
questions.explanation: At scale, networks fail partially; systems must handle partitions. The practical trade-off is between consistency (C) and availability (A) while partitioned—not whether partitions occur.

questions.question: During a network partition, a system that rejects writes on minority partitions to prevent divergence prioritizes:
options: Consistency over availability for those partitions
Availability over consistency for all clients globally
Durability over latency only, with no consistency implications
Manageability over cost exclusively
questions.explanation: Choosing not to serve ambiguous writes during a split favors consistency (avoiding conflicting states) at the cost of availability on unreachable segments—classic CP behavior.

questions.question: Consistent hashing is preferred over naive modulo hashing (key % N) when scaling a cluster because:
options: Adding or removing nodes remaps only a fraction of keys instead of nearly all keys
It guarantees strong consistency on every read
It eliminates the need for replication entirely
It forces all keys into alphabetical order on one server
questions.explanation: AOSA uses consistent hashing for caches and shards: modulo redistribution reshuffles most keys when N changes; consistent hashing bounds remapping—foundational to DHT-style lookup.

questions.question: A Distributed Hash Table (DHT) at the architectural level provides:
options: Decentralized key-to-node lookup so clients can find which peer stores a key without a central directory
A single SQL primary that stores all metadata
Lossless compression of multi-terabyte logs
Automatic strong serializability for financial transactions
questions.explanation: DHTs combine consistent hashing with peer routing so responsibility for keys is distributed; lookups traverse O(log N) hops rather than one global catalog.

questions.question: In leader-follower (primary-replica) replication, writes typically:
options: Go to the leader, which replicates to followers for read scaling and failover
Are applied independently on every replica with no coordination
Are forbidden; only reads are allowed
Must be routed through a CDN edge cache
questions.explanation: Leader-follower centralizes writes on one node (ordering, conflict avoidance) while followers serve reads or standby for promotion—common in databases and log pipelines.

questions.question: Multi-leader (multi-primary) replication is often chosen for multi-datacenter writes but introduces:
options: Write conflicts that require detection and resolution across leaders
Impossibility of any reads from followers
Mandatory synchronous cross-region quorum on every SELECT
Elimination of network partitions by design
questions.explanation: Multiple writable leaders reduce cross-region write latency but concurrent updates to the same record can conflict—needing LWW, versioning, or application merge rules.

questions.question: In leaderless replication (e.g., Dynamo-style), a read quorum of R and write quorum of W over N replicas can provide strong-ish behavior when:
options: R + W > N so reads and writes overlap on enough replicas
R = 1 and W = 1 regardless of N
N = 1 and replicas are disabled
All reads bypass replicas and hit object storage only
questions.explanation: Quorum intersection (R + W > N) increases chance reads see latest write; tunable R/W trades latency, availability, and staleness.

questions.question: Hash partitioning by user_id spreads load evenly when access is uniform but can still create hot partitions when:
options: A few keys receive disproportionate traffic (celebrity users, viral content)
All keys are UUIDs with no semantic meaning
Replicas outnumber primaries
The hash function uses consistent hashing
questions.explanation: Hashing balances skewed key distributions in expectation, not in every workload—extreme popularity concentrates load on one partition regardless of hash quality.

questions.question: Range partitioning (e.g., by timestamp or key range) simplifies range queries but often causes hot partitions because:
options: Recent or 'latest' keys cluster on the tail partition receiving most writes/reads
Range partitions cannot be replicated
Hash functions are illegal with sorted keys
Load balancers cannot route to range shards
questions.explanation: Time-series and 'newest first' access patterns hammer the high-end range shard—motivating sub-splitting, salting, or hybrid partitioning strategies.

questions.question: Which mitigation best addresses a hot partition caused by one extremely popular entity?
options: Split or replicate that key's traffic across multiple shards (e.g., salted sub-keys)
Disable caching for that key only
Move all partitions to a single larger machine
Switch from replication to batch MapReduce for all reads
questions.explanation: When hashing isn't enough, architects fan out hot keys (replica reads, sub-shards, dedicated cache tier) so one partition isn't the sole bottleneck.

questions.question: Eventual consistency is often acceptable for social media 'like' counts but problematic for bank balances because:
options: Users notice incorrect financial balances immediately; stale likes are tolerable briefly
Likes require synchronous disk fsync on every continent
Bank balances are always served from CDN edge caches
Eventual consistency only applies to message queues, not databases
questions.explanation: AOSA notes writes may propagate slowly (eventual consistency); product requirements dictate tolerance—financial correctness needs stronger guarantees than approximate counters.

questions.question: Strong consistency across replicas typically costs more latency because:
options: Reads or writes must coordinate with multiple nodes (quorum/leader acknowledgment) before replying
Strong consistency disables all indexes
Clients must poll a message queue for every read
Data must be stored only on spinning disks
questions.explanation: Linearizable or serializable behavior requires synchronization—extra RTTs, leader election, or quorum—trading speed for a single coherent view.

questions.question: Idempotent operations are critical in distributed systems with retries because:
options: Duplicate delivery of the same request does not change state beyond the first successful application
They guarantee zero network partitions
They remove the need for any persistent storage
They force exactly-once delivery at the transport layer
questions.explanation: At-least-once delivery plus timeouts means clients retry; idempotent handlers (or dedup keys) prevent double-charging, double-insert, or duplicate side effects.

questions.question: Exactly-once processing semantics in practice are usually achieved by combining:
options: At-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers and deduplication or transactional offsets
UDP without acknowledgments
A single global mutex for all microservices
Disabling all client retries and timeouts
questions.explanation: True exactly-once end-to-end is hard; systems approximate it with durable logs, idempotent writes, and dedup—conceptual interview answer, not magic network guarantees.

questions.question: In the MapReduce pattern, the map phase at the architectural level:
options: Processes input records in parallel on workers, emitting intermediate key-value pairs
Combines all reducer outputs into the final dashboard UI
Joins click and query logs in real time with sub-second latency
Elects a single leader for all database writes
questions.explanation: Map workers apply a function per record in parallel; output is shuffled/sorted by key before reducers aggregate—batch-oriented horizontal scale (NALSD AdWords iteration).

questions.question: The shuffle phase between map and reduce exists primarily to:
options: Group all intermediate values by key so each reducer receives the complete set for its keys
Encrypt data at rest on HDFS
Replace the need for consistent hashing in caches
Serve interactive OLTP queries under 10 ms
questions.explanation: Shuffle transfers and sorts mapper output by key—often the expensive network step—so reducers can compute aggregates (counts, sums, joins) per key.

questions.question: NALSD rejects small-batch MapReduce for AdWords CTR freshness because:
options: Non-overlapping batches can place a query in one batch and its click in another, preventing joins
MapReduce cannot run on more than one machine
MapReduce always provides sub-second dashboard latency
Click logs cannot be stored on disk
questions.explanation: MapReduce excels at large self-contained batches; arbitrary log-arrival ordering breaks join correctness when batches are tiny and non-overlapping—motivating streaming LogJoiner design.

questions.question: At the architectural level, breadth-first graph traversal on a partitioned graph most often:
options: Passes messages between workers holding adjacent partitions each superstep/layer
Loads the entire graph into one machine's L1 cache
Uses only a CDN without an origin store
Requires O(1) storage regardless of edge count
questions.explanation: Distributed BFS (Pregel/Giraph-style thinking): each round exchanges frontier vertices across partition boundaries—communication per layer dominates, matching email graph pipeline topics.
